description: How C++ manages memory and resources: pointers vs references, stack vs heap, new/delete and their pitfalls, then RAII and the smart pointers that make manual memory management obsolete, with a first look at move semantics.
primer:
## What this set covers
The distinctive part of C++: you control memory, and the modern idioms (RAII, smart pointers) that make that control safe. Every card traces back to this page.

## Pointers & references
- A **pointer** stores an address: `int* p = &x;`. `&x` is the **address-of** operator; `*p` **dereferences** (reads/writes the target); `p->m` is `(*p).m`. A pointer can be **reseated** and can be **null**.
- The null literal is **`nullptr`** (typed; preferred over `NULL`/`0`).
- A **reference** must be initialised, can never be rebound, and **cannot be null** — prefer it when you always have a valid object and never rebind; use a pointer when you need to rebind or represent 'nothing'.
- The `&` symbol means **address-of** as a prefix operator (`&x`) but **'reference to'** in a type (`int&`).
- `const int* p` = pointer to a const value (can't change `*p`, can reseat `p`); `int* const p` = const pointer (can change `*p`, can't reseat). Read right-to-left.
- **Pointer arithmetic**: `p + 1` advances by one *element* (`sizeof(T)`), and `a[i]` is `*(a + i)`. A raw array **decays to a bare pointer** when passed to a function, losing its length.

## Stack vs heap
- **Stack**: automatic locals, created at declaration and destroyed (destructor run) at the **end of scope**, in reverse order — fast, automatic, size-limited.
- **Heap (free store)**: `new T` allocates + constructs and returns a pointer; `delete p` destroys + frees. Arrays use `new T[n]` / `delete[] p`. **Every `new` must match exactly one `delete`.**
- Pitfalls: a **memory leak** is heap memory never freed (the owning pointer was lost); a **dangling pointer** points at already-freed/destroyed storage; a **double free** (two `delete`s) is undefined behaviour. After `delete`, set the pointer to `nullptr` (deleting null is a safe no-op). A pointer to a local **dangles** once the function returns.

## RAII
- **Resource Acquisition Is Initialisation**: acquire a resource in a constructor, release it in the destructor. Because destructors run automatically at scope exit — **including during exception stack-unwinding** — the resource is always released. This is why RAII is exception-safe where bare `new`/`delete` is not.

## Smart pointers
- **`std::unique_ptr`**: sole ownership, **move-only** (can't copy), frees its object on destruction. Create with **`std::make_unique<T>(args)`** (C++14). The default — cheapest and clearest. Return one **by value** to transfer ownership (a factory).
- **`std::shared_ptr`**: **reference-counted** shared ownership; the object is freed when the **last** owner dies. Create with `std::make_shared<T>(args)` (one allocation, exception-safe, no raw `new`). Building two shared_ptrs from the *same* raw pointer gives two counts → **double free**.
- **`std::weak_ptr`**: a **non-owning observer** of a shared_ptr's object (doesn't change the count); call `.lock()` to get a shared_ptr if still alive. It **breaks reference cycles**: two shared_ptrs holding each other never reach count 0 (a leak) — make one link a weak_ptr.
- **Prefer unique_ptr first**; use shared_ptr only when ownership is genuinely shared.

## Move semantics (intro)
- **`std::move`** is just a **cast to an rvalue** — it does nothing at runtime by itself, but signals 'you may steal my resources', letting a move constructor/assignment **transfer** internals (a pointer/buffer) instead of deep-copying. The moved-from object is valid but unspecified.
- This makes returning big objects cheap and is exactly why move-only types like `unique_ptr` work: transfer ownership with `sink(std::move(p));` (afterwards `p` is null).
